Thank you for the bug report. I believe that I have just fixed this.

Underscores are supposed to be stripped when they are in number values (integers, floats) but I put the stripping too early to be sure that the value is a number. I added a check to make sure that I only remove underscores in values which start with a digit (so not strings or arrays or things of that sort).

Feel free to reopen this if the issue persists.

AFAIK it's not that common for vimscript plugin authors to care about scrubbing unused variables (and false positives) from their projects yet so I wouldn't expect you'd see such conventions used much in vimscript projects in the wild. @imrn: The problem is that per-operation timeouts are almost never what you actually want. You usually want to impose timeouts on higher level actions like "this HTTP request" not "reading the next byte from this socket". So timeout parameters aren't very useful in practice, and involve a huge amount of extra code in curio, + extra code in all user APIs if the user API wants to follow the convention and implement a timeout parameter too. Better just to consistently leave it out and stick with the context manager approach.

Was thinking about this more.  I bet you could simplify it further by simply putting all of the coroutines in a deque.  Then await them after you call the Tkinter update() method.  For example::

    pending = deque()

    def some_callback():
        ...
        pending.append(spawn(some_task())

    async def main():
         ...
         update()     # Tkinter update method
         while pending:
                await pending.popleft())

That's a bit rough, but I think it would work just like the Queue method.  Maybe even better.

Hi Jacob, 

The volatile attribute is intended to be applied at only the libraries linked with the full path without the linker intrusion (-L and -l switches). The reason is that the libraries linked with their full path are more likely a *poor* library rather than a **system** library: track the change of a system library is not a FoBiS.py aim, when this happens just type `FoBiS.py clean ; FoBiS.py build`, whereas a volatile library can change suddendly and it is nice that FoBiS.py check them for us. Anyhow, introducing a volatile system library should be simple, if you want I will do it later in the morning.

The interdependency *projects* (I am really thinking to a fobos files dependency, the notion of projects is currently unknown for FoBiS.py) is in my radar, but I cannot say when it will be ready. However, we can start to discuss some details. I am thinking to something like this:

+ consider 2 fobos (project) files placed elsewhere (not necessarly in the same directory;
+ fobos_lib is the project file for building a library;
+ fobos_prog is the fobos of the main program using that library;
+ we want to build the main program and let FoBiS to care about all dependncies in between to keep uptodate the library progect...

In this scenario, I thinking to add a special option into the fobos like the following (I am using the default section just as an example, but it can be used into any defined modes)
```ini
[default]
dependon = ./relative_path_to/fobos_lib:static
compiler = ...
....
```
Note that I have specified not only the fobos file but also the actual mode (section) that in this case is `static` but it can be any modes defined into fobos_lib (in case it is omitted the default or the first one is used). Now, if we do `FoBiS.py build -f fobos_prog` the first thing it does will be `FoBiS.py build -f fobos_lib -mode static` into the correct directory and then it continues with the building of the program. In this scenario this feature can be used also from the command line avoiding the fobos_prog, but the fobos_lib is still necessary.

What do you think about this feature (that is your :-)?

@Tobychev What do you think?

See you later.

You're talking about something else: indeed LeastSquares is _also_ an objective. What I'm referring to here is a symbolic representation of the chi-squared of a model. The need for such an object arises in the context of indexed variables because explicit symbolic sums might need to be performed in some situations, but I made this a seperate issue because it might be worth doing it anyway because conceptually it might be nice to have anyway.

You are right that the name might be confusing, so it should be named differently. Or perhaps even better, it could be a convenience method that takes a model as input and which returns a chi2 of that model as output.

To answer your question, I think those methods are used by the exact solvers `LinearLeastSquares` and `NonLinearLeastSquares` but I'm not sure anymore. That's something which also has to be looked into, those objects are pretty outdated API wise.

Okay, unfortunately, updating to mathstats 0.2.5 and retrying the command did not work, resulting in exactly the same error.

Here's the problematic code: https://github.com/ksahlin/BESST/blob/master/BESST/GenerateOutput.py#L63

Specifically, this part of the code is where the problem is coming from:
```Python
round(cont_obj.coverage/param.mean_coverage,0)
```
Replicating the error in IPython:
```Python
In [10]: round(1.0/0,0)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ZeroDivisionError                         Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-10-87eba751f4c6> in <module>()
----> 1 round(1.0/0,0)

ZeroDivisionError: float division by zero

In [11]: round(1/0.0,0)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ZeroDivisionError                         Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-11-11b0b1965d8a> in <module>()
----> 1 round(1/0.0,0)

ZeroDivisionError: float division by zero
```

Therefore `param.mean_coverage` must be set to zero for some reason. I confirmed this by looking at the `Statistics.txt` file after the program exits:

```
Parsing BAM file...

Reads with too large insert size from "USEFUL READS" (filtered out):  2344045
Initial number of edges in G (the graph with large contigs):  0
Initial number of edges in G_prime (the full graph of all contigs before removal of repats):  5304
Number of duplicated reads indicated and removed:  24399
Mean coverage before filtering out extreme observations =  498.836133553
Std dev of coverage before filtering out extreme observations=  1202.33071831
Mean coverage after filtering =  0.0
Std coverage after filtering =  0.0
Length of longest contig in calc of coverage:  5642686
Length of shortest contig in calc of coverage:  1327
Detecting repeats..
```

This parameter is set in 2 places:
1) https://github.com/ksahlin/BESST/blob/f38c8d27bb189da81a8f8ba27dffb305f1bcca5e/BESST/Parameter.py#L53
2) https://github.com/ksahlin/BESST/blob/f38c8d27bb189da81a8f8ba27dffb305f1bcca5e/BESST/CreateGraph.py#L253

I believe that really only #2 matters, where we see `param.mean_coverage = mean_cov`. The `mean_cov` variable comes from the previous line of code: https://github.com/ksahlin/BESST/blob/f38c8d27bb189da81a8f8ba27dffb305f1bcca5e/BESST/CreateGraph.py#L252
```Python
mean_cov, std_dev_cov = CalculateMeanCoverage(Contigs, Information, param)
```

This leads us to the CalculateMeanCoverage function: https://github.com/ksahlin/BESST/blob/f38c8d27bb189da81a8f8ba27dffb305f1bcca5e/BESST/CreateGraph.py#L883

It looks like something is going wrong in this section of the function: https://github.com/ksahlin/BESST/blob/f38c8d27bb189da81a8f8ba27dffb305f1bcca5e/BESST/CreateGraph.py#L903

The `mean_cov` variable is set again here: https://github.com/ksahlin/BESST/blob/f38c8d27bb189da81a8f8ba27dffb305f1bcca5e/BESST/CreateGraph.py#L908

It is dependent on the `filtered_list` object which is calculated by the `RemoveOutliers` function: https://github.com/ksahlin/BESST/blob/f38c8d27bb189da81a8f8ba27dffb305f1bcca5e/BESST/CreateGraph.py#L925

Nothing really jumps out at me in the code as to where the error is originating, but perhaps it will be more obvious to you, Kristoffer! Hopefully my bit of digging around is helpful to you. I'd like to get this fixed ASAP, because I need to finish scaffolding this assembly! :P

There's a difference between a division by zero and not checking the length before calling an external API. The outcome of a division by zero is well defined. The outcome of reading uninitialized memory is way less predicable.

Everything may seem to work as expected, and under some hard-to-define, hard-to-reproduce conditions, the application crashes or may expose a vulnerability. This is not great.

Even if implemented as simple `assert` statements, I'd suggest checking parameter lengths.

You should be sure that ``sys.path`` does include the site-packages directory where ldap3 is installed: This is what I get on my notebook, with an environment similar to yours:

```
>>> from sys import path
>>> print(path)
['', 'C:\\Python\\Python36\\python36.zip', 'C:\\Python\\Python36\\DLLs', 'C:\\Python\\Python36\\lib', 'C:\\Python\\Python36', 'C:\\Python\\Python36\\lib\\site-packages']
```
and the ldap3 library is in  'C:\\Python\\Python36\\lib\\site-packages'

When running in IDLE i get a different path list:

```

You can also use txdbus as usual (with *Deferred* objects) within an asyncio loop:
```
import asyncio
from asyncio.tasks import ensure_future
from twisted.internet import asyncioreactor

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
asyncio.set_event_loop(loop)
asyncioreactor.install(eventloop=loop)

from twisted.internet import reactor, defer, task
from txdbus import error, client

@defer.inlineCallbacks
def show_desktop_notification():
    '''
    Displays "Hello World!" in a desktop notification window for 3 seconds
    '''
    con = yield client.connect(reactor, 'session')

    notifier = yield con.getRemoteObject('org.freedesktop.Notifications',
                                         '/org/freedesktop/Notifications')

    nid = yield notifier.callRemote('Notify',
                                    'Example Application',
                                    0,
                                    '',
                                    'Tx DBus Example',
                                    'Hello World!',
                                    [], dict(),
                                    3000)

reactor.callWhenRunning(show_desktop_notification)
reactor.startRunning()
loop.run_forever()
```

There is no guarantee that the dictionaries will order keys in the same fashion across Python versions. In your case, it looks like the order is different resulting in a different token.

Example:
```
>>> import jwt.utils
>>> jwt.utils.base64url_decode('eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9')
'{"typ":"JWT","alg":"HS256"}'
>>> jwt.utils.base64url_decode('eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9')
'{"alg":"HS256","typ":"JWT"}'
>>> 
```

You can see here that if we decode the header part of the JWT to the resulting string, we have equivalent but different JSON. In both cases the token will still verify properly and you shouldn't have any issues.

Interesting note: ECDSA introduces a cryptographically random curve point so if you use this family of algorithms, you'll have different tokens generated with the same inputs every time.

Hi guys!
I had a similar problem under Windows 7: after half a day of bothering around with simply installing libtiff package (oh my!!!!) I stucked the other half of day with the PATH error, that was already mentioned on top of the present issue. My solution was quite simple - and it indeed embarrass me a little. But here is what I did:

The error is raised by libtiff_ctypes.py (originated in C:\Python27\Lib\site-packages\libtiff\), line 41 because the tiff library is not found. In line 29 you can find the location WHERE the ctypes-module is searching for the tiff library. Since I am not a friend of changing tested and in principle working python modules I copied the location path from line 29 and reinstallled the tiff package - or GnuWin32 application - into this directory. Additionally I had to add C:\Program Files\GnuWin32\bin to the PATH environment variables. For newbies: This is done by the following
Go to Control Panel -> System, on the left side click on "Advanced system settings" and enter Admin-password. Then in slide "Advanced" click on "Environment Variables" (at the bottom of the window) and in "System Variables" you search for an entry called "PATH". Here, click on "Edit" (nothing else, don't delete!!!!!!), go to the end of the line and add a semicolon and the necessary path. Confirm with "OK" etc. In principle under Windows 7 it should already work, evtl you will have to reboot the PC once

By the way: the libtiff3.dll-copy thing, recommended by Kevin, didn't work for me as well ;)

Cheers!
Alice

Good point. This is something tricky that comes up a lot when you have files with multiple features in one that overlap a single feature in another and then try to make a Venn diagram -- which has no category for "multiple hits".

The reason you run into this issue is that the `BedTool.__add__` method uses the `u=True` argument to `intersectBed`.  (`__subtract__` uses `v=True` to be symmetrical).  If you have nested features (2 features in *b* that overlap a feature in *a*) then you'll run into this issue.  

For example, what should the 2-way Venn diagram look like for these files?  It's not really defined what should go in that middle overlap section of the diagram:

    a.bed  -------------        --------------------------
    b.bed      -----------               -----     ------


Using `u=True`:

$ intersectBed -a a.bed -b b.bed -u | wc -l 
2

$ intersectBed -a b.bed -b a.bed -u | wc -l
3

Not using `u=True` results in another issue -- the total number of features for `a` overlapping with `b` is greater than the number of features in `a` in the first place:

$ intersectBed -a a.bed -b b.bed | wc -l
3

$ intersectBed -a b.bed -b a.bed | wc -l
3

This latter version can result in the sum of the each circle in the Venn diagram being huge and having no relation to the original number of features.

Unfortunately, Venn diagrams aren't ideal for overlapping cases like this.  In my applications it made sense to use the `u=True` case.  Do you think it makes more sense to use the `u=False`?  

If you'd like to play around, you can subclass BedTool and overwrite its `__add__` method to do what you want:

    class MyBedTool(BedTool):
        def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
            BedTool.__init__(self, *args, **kwargs)
        def __add__(self, other):
            return self.intersect(other)

Any suggestions on how to improve this?  I'd imagine others will run into this as well, so I'll at least have to add this explanation to the docs.

In general, Python lets you provide additional keyword arguments as a dictionary, where keys are strings, and use the `**` unpacking syntax in the function call. There are several cases in BEDTools where arguments are Python reserved keywords, and in each case the workaround looks like this:

```python
kwargs = {'is': True}
intersect = a.pair_to_pair(b, type='either', **kwargs)
```

Notice the syntax highlighting affects `type` there (which is a Python built-in), but at least it won't give a syntax error like `is`. You could also do this:

```python
intersect = a.pair_t_pair(b, **{'type': 'either', 'is': True})
```

Try using assign() instead of subscribe(). This will allow you to seek
without poll(). The reason this works is that subscribe forces the client
to check metadata for the topic within the poll() call. Assigning
partitions manually does not require fetching metadata so you can seek
immediately.

I think I grok now the root of the problems here: subclassing. `Symbol` and `BaseElement` should not be IMHO subclasses of `Expression`. These are literals, terminals, leaves. And probably neither should `Function`.
An expression contains a collection of these. But a `Function` alone is not a `type of` expression, this is a `part of`. And while an expression can be composed of a single `Symbol` and `BaseElement` these are also a `part of` and a `type of` expression. So subclassing everything from `Expression` does make sense to avoid duplicating code, but does not make sense logically IMHO. 

There should be IMHO:
1. an algebra defined by the implementation of the base elements, functions, symbol and tokenizer
2. from a defined algebra you can build expressions. And expressions holds a tree of base elements, functions, symbol. 
3. While functions have arguments, Symbols and base elements not. So expressions do not have have arguments. They hold a single expression root object, not a sequence of args. 
4. Symbols have an associated object, but Expressions, Functions and BaseElements do not (though eventually a symbol and base element are the same: and the associated object of a  base element is `True` or `False`. And Symbols could be defined as being TRUE or FALSE too possibly, as opposed to only True today.
5. NOT has a single argument, and AND and OR have a sequence of arguments. Each with markedly different methods.

I may be restating mostly the obvious here.... the main point is that subclassing everything from Expression may not make sense and may be mixing apple and oranges in a the same type. I will try to see if injection some composition and a different class structure may help. 

The second point is that to allow any kind of customization, you cannot use the bare classes right away. You need to work within the confines of an algebra definition. The class hierarchy should not be what defines the algebra IMHO. You need to define an algebra first first eventually using the defaults. But the mere instantiation of one of the elements, functions or symbol outside of a defined algebra should not be allowed.

I hope this will become clearer when I push some exploration branch.

This is not a pyodbc issue. It is a more general ODBC issue, possibly related to the specific behaviour of the Access ODBC driver. I was able to reproduce similar results using VBScript and ADODB.

A valid ODBC DSN already contains the `DRIVER=` information, so if you want to establish a connection using `DSN=` then you should specify *just* the `DSN=`, not `DSN=` and `DRIVER=`. If you want to specify the driver name and the database path then you should use `DRIVER=` and `DBQ=`.

@ruiyuanlu An empty file is not a PPTX file. A PPTX file is a zip archive with a prescribed set of contents.

An "empty" PPTX file is simply one that has no slides. It does still however have quite a bit of content, like the slide masters, theme, slide layouts, etc.

`python-pptx` can only operate on PPTX files. Calling `Presentation()` with no parameters causes an internal default template PPTX file to be opened. You can create your own starting template by taking the PPTX file of your choice and deleting all its slides and saving it.

You may want to spend some time with the documentation here that explains these concepts: https://python-pptx.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user/presentations.html

The thing to bear in mind is that the modules have been created by different users at different times.  The project is now at a point where we need to start making things so that they behave in similar ways so that users can quickly configure them etc, allow functionality to be shared across all modules.  we also need to respect users and not break there setups.

My opinion and this is just my opinion is that we should have standard config namings

`format_...` these describe the formats are processed by the formatter so have all that functionality.
Ideally modules should have a single `format` but in some cases this is not possible

`icon_...` used to allow user icons/text used for placeholders

`button_...` used to select what button performs an action

this is a long process and sometimes we need to make changes to core functionality to allow modules to be updated, eg we can now allow configuration options to be added/removed/renamed using the deprecation stuff.  For some modules/users needs we have have had to add new formatter options for example.  Sometimes changes to modules will happen over a period of time rather than all at once.  But when naming things (probably the hardest aspect of coding by a long way)  it is good to think things through as undoing these changes is painful.

@andyr  
> This means sample data or cleanup if that stuff is stored in a database/persistence layer right?

It means that if you run a set of API calls to set up data (test users, test user data, mock records) for your test scenario or remove results afterward, you can create an import for that.  Similarly you can create a testset import to run API actions that delete the test data afterward (and test this worked correctly).

You just can't rely on the PyRestTest contexts to store variables/generators between testsets, because each one gets a fresh context.  

> One of the simplest use cases is that I want to create a yaml file to store constants (api key, etc) that are used in every file (over 100). 

There's two ways to handle this currently.  The most succinct one is to pass them as command-line arguments (it takes an inline YAML argument 'vars').  The feature is fairly new and AFAIK hasn't been used yet, so it may have issues, but it's worth trying.  In this case, you'd run pyresttest from a shellscript that builds up this argument (possibly creating a shell variable, i.e. $PYRRESTEST_VARS that is used to store it). 

Option 2 is a bit uglier - use environment variables to store individual values, and set them by a shellscipt before testing.  You define named [env_variable generators](https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/blob/master/advanced_guide.md#generators-listing) in the testconfigs, and then do a generator_bind element in each test that needs them.  This involves a lot of ugly boilerplate though.

Added https://github.com/svanoort/pyresttest/issues/178 to enable imports to better aid this.  You're the second user to request it, and now I have a plan.  Dynamic scoping #79 and make-everything-dynamic #101 are probably the issues that will assist with this too.

Having been implementing some of the image module, in particular the simulation of images, I've re-read this and realised it is extremely confusing the way I've implemented it. The code keeps track of the background sky level, which can be used for generating Poisson noise and estimating the noise in an image.

The reality is, provided the image is in electrons per second (allowing you can easily 'scale' the background sky subtraction), there is no point keeping track of the sky level in the code and it is confusing that noise methods can use it to estimate the noise or generate noise.

Therefore, I will remove sky level from the code.

Furthermore, whilst we won't use BackgroundNoise or PoissonNoise for the analysis, we will have them as part of the imaging module as reduced data may be in this form and thus be necessary for producing the BaselineNoise. We may ultimately wish to revert to the other forms of noise map scaling too.

Hi @OliverGavin,

```
evdev.uinput.UInputError: input device not opened - cannot read capabilities
```

`UInput()` creates a new input device in `/dev/input/` that you most likely don't have access to. Adding yourself to the `input` group should fix that. Another option is to add an udev group that changes the attributes of the device. For example:

```
# /etc/udev/rules.d/10-local.rules
KERNEL=="event*", NAME="input/%k", MODE="660", GROUP="input", OWNER="oliver"
```

Reload udev with:

```
udevadm control --reload-rules 
udevadm trigger
```

You could do the same thing with `uinput`, though I'm not sure what the exact rule syntax to match `/dev/uinput` would be.

Going deeper in SNORT rules i foud that they use a more particular pattern to include metadata in the `msg` field:

- the first uppercase word is the category
- the first uppercase word may be a dash-separated word, in this case the first part is the main category while the second seems to be a subcategory
- if the category is DELETED the second word is the previous category of the rule

With in mind I think that a parser to extract metadata from `msg` field cannot work if we did not tell it what convention is in use. So i think that this feature, if approved, should be used by giving an explicit parameter and should not be enabled by default (to avoid unnecessary and error prone parsing).

def parse(buf, group=None, msg_metadata=None):

Yeah, that is a limitation of this approach. I think it's actually a good thing in the cases I've seen - for instance, it means that your `validate_start_and_end_dates_in_correct_order()` function does not have to worry about the fact that the inputs may not even be valid dates. If the second-tier validator got run in any case, you'd want to write it with guarding logic about, for instance, the case where one date is valid but not the other, etc, so that you didn't end up throwing all kinds of exceptions. If the dates aren't valid in the first place, the more complex validator isn't going to do you any good anyway.

I suppose you could write a different version of `All` that goes ahead and tries all the validators even if an earlier one failed, but that's not how it works out of the box. The output of each validator in an `All` is passed to the next so that downstream validators can take advantage of any validation and coercion that might be happening upstream.

ChatterBot currently doesn't support form-like information gathering in the core. Typically this is a somewhat specialized process that depends highly on the type of information being gathered and the circumstances under which the information is needed. So yes, if a someone wants to gather specific information from a user, they must code this logic themselves.

ChatterBot does allow you to [create your own logic adapters](http://chatterbot.readthedocs.io/en/latest/logic/create-a-logic-adapter.html). Typically, logic adapters are where responses are selected, but you can also write your own modified ones that look for specific inputs to respond to. In the case of your example of ordering flowers, it would be a simple enough case to check if the user's input says something close to "Place my order of flowers" then make an API call to place the order.

`asynqp` is missing a feature - passive declarations of channels and queues - but I think you are also misunderstanding the API.

`asynqp` *does* raise channel errors as Python exceptions, when you use the library correctly. You should be using [`channel.declare_exchange`](http://asynqp.readthedocs.org/en/v0.4/reference.html#asynqp.Channel.declare_exchange) to declare your exchange (and awaiting it using `yield from`). Don't use `channel.sender` - it's an internal and undocumented part of `asynqp`.

Now, `channel.declare_exchange` doesn't presently support a `passive` parameter, but that should be pretty easy to add now - I just didn't get around to it. I'm on holiday at the moment, but I'll accept a pull request (you just need to add a parameter [here](https://github.com/benjamin-hodgson/asynqp/blob/master/src/asynqp/channel.py#L40) and pass the argument down to [here](https://github.com/benjamin-hodgson/asynqp/blob/master/src/asynqp/channel.py#L336)).

However I'm also confused as to what you're trying to achieve in the first place. Why do you want to publish messages to an exchange which might not exist?

Do you mean you want to extract whatever text is found on the image without validating it for compliance with an MRZ format?

Of course everything is possible, however for many practical situations I've encountered this will severely reduce the utility of the method, as it is not uncommon for the (current) algorithm to locate multiple potential boxes with text on a page at its first stage. Unless these are filtered by checking which ones resemble a MRZ you won't get much useful results.

Consider reading the definition of `passporteye.mrz.image.MRZPipeline`  and the few classes referenced by it. Perhaps you'll find that it is fairly easy to attempt your own variations of the pipeline which include or exclude some of the steps, so that you'd be able to devise a version that suits your particular data and needs.

Asciimatics doesn't have anything like that built-in.  The simplest way to achieve what you want is to create a psuedoterminal (a.k.a. a pty) for your telnetserver and then use that to drive a separate process running asciimatics.

In a bit more detail, you could write your whole asciimatics application as normal, prove that it works when run locally by you.  You then wrap that inside your telnet server using a pty.  Sample python code for using pty is available here: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/4022600/python-pty-fork-how-does-it-work

If you have to run it all inside a single process, then you'll need to create a new Screen implementation that emulates the terminal - e.g. start from the existing _CursesScreen, but rip out all references to curses and start using the terminal emulation of your choice and printing that directly to your socket stream.  There might be a package that already emulates a terminal for you if you really want to try this...  I'd recommend the first approach, though.

Hey @jamesdj, we do offer tests for n>2 actually: 

```
def pairwise_logrank_test(event_durations, groups, event_observed=None,   alpha=0.95, t_0=-1, bonferroni=True)
    Perform the logrank test pairwise for all n>2 unique groups (use the more appropriate logrank_test for n=2).
    We have to be careful here: if there are n groups, then there are n*(n-1)/2 pairs -- so many pairs increase
    the chance that here will exist a significantly different pair purely by chance. For this reason, we use the
    Bonferroni correction (rewight the alpha value higher to accomidate the multiple tests).
    Parameters:
      event_durations: a (n,) numpy array the (partial) lifetimes of all individuals
      groups: a (n,) numpy array of unique group labels for each individual.
      event_observed: a (n,) numpy array of event_observed events: 1 if observed death, 0 if censored. Defaults
          to all observed.
      alpha: the level of signifiance desired.
      t_0: the final time to compare the series' up to. Defaults to all.
      bonferroni: If true, uses the Bonferroni correction to compare the M=n(n-1)/2 pairs, i.e alpha = alpha/M
            See (here)[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bonferroni_correction].
      kwargs: add keywords and meta-data to the experiment summary.
    Returns:
        R: a (n,n) dataframe of StatisticalResults (None on the diagonal)
```

and 
```
def multivariate_logrank_test(event_durations, groups, event_observed=None,
                              alpha=0.95, t_0=-1, **kwargs):
    """
    This test is a generalization of the logrank_test: it can deal with n>2 populations (and should
      be equal when n=2):
    H_0: all event series are from the same generating processes
    H_A: there exist atleast one group that differs from the other.
    Parameters:
      event_durations: a (n,) numpy array the (partial) lifetimes of all individuals
      groups: a (n,) numpy array of unique group labels for each individual.
      event_observed: a (n,) numpy array of event observations: 1 if observed death, 0 if censored. Defaults
          to all observed.
      alpha: the level of significance desired.
      t_0: the final time to compare the series' up to. Defaults to all.
      kwargs: add keywords and meta-data to the experiment summary.
    Returns
      results: a StatisticalResult object with properties 'p_value', 'summary', 'test_statistic', 'test_result'
    """
```

This is pretty interesting. Thanks for the report!

In all honesty, this project needs a bit of Django upgrade love. I haven't been doing much Django for about a year and should probably look for another maintainer for this project long term.

I have a hunch that the attributes is returning this filter object because something in Django that used to be eager is now lazy, or that this change is introduced from using Python 3 rather than 2. If I recall correctly, this project works for Python 2 and 3, but I wouldn't be surprised if this wasn't the underlying issue. I think what attributes does is basically like details but only taking the keys, which is now lazy in Python 3. We could probably just wrap this in list() to make it work.

The variables tag and details tags have values I would expect, though I don't remember seeing bound_blocks before in particular. This seems to have objects that wagtail defines so I suspect that's where this bit is coming from.

There could be something that makes details output more useful. For instance, methods and functions appear as "routine" rather than the built-in representation. I don't see how you'd do something like this for wagtail data though, beyond adding in an extension mechanism where a function from the settings gets called with the output to act as a transformation to what you want. For something like wagtail, this might be a really useful feature.

In short, I think the main crux of the issue here, and the fix, would be to see what attributes is doing and possibly forcing evaluation there with list().

> The advanced indexing I meant is indexing an NDArray with indices
> containing lists, np.ndarray, NDArray etc., instead of integers or slices.
> In your example, a[[0, -1]] is advanced indexing, and it internally calls
> gather_nd operator which only supports non-negative indices. a[(0, -1)]
> is equivalent to a[0, -1] and is basic indexing, which internally calls
> slice operator and it supports non-negative indices.
>
> In your last example, both xs[(a, b)] and xs[a][b] are basic indexing if a
> and b are integers or slices. Something like a[[0, 1]] is slower than a[0,
> 1] is because the index [0, 1] has to be prepared in Python as an NDArray
> and passed to the backend operator, while (0, 1) has much less overhead
> to be passed to the backend operator.

@oliw it is definitely production ready, we have been using it for a few months now with no issues. It is just not patched by default when calling `patch_all` with no arguments.

The typical reason why you won't see any traces is because a "service" isn't defined for the spans created by the `requests` patching.

If you can check the logs for the trace agent you might see messages "Dropping trace no service was set". I don't remember exactly what the message says, but it is a pretty obvious message.

```
tail -f /var/log/datadog/trace-agent.log
```

Any spans that are created without a service defined (or whose parent spans do not have a service defined) will get ignored.

There are a few options to get around this:

```python
from ddtrace import tracer
import requests

# Wrap a function
@tracer.wrap(service='my-app'):
def make_http_call():
    res = requests.get('https://httpbin.org/status/200')

# Context manager
with tracer.trace('trace.name', service='my-app'):
    res = requests.get('https://httpbin.org/status/200')
```

I hope this helps point you in the right direction.

Hey @iroth,

Using `entry.update(attributes)` will replace the content of the entry with the content of the attributes sent.

The easiest way update content, is to do it using the setters for each property and then calling `entry.save()`. For example:

```python
entry.fieldA = "some value"
entry.fieldB = "something else"
entry.save()
```

By doing this, you avoid having to explicitly mention all of the fields on the `update` call as it is handled internally, keeping all the values in the fields.

Hope this helps,

Cheers

Apparently I was wrong about the `release()` method on the pure-python implementation of the Git database. However, there is a git-command based implementation which uses `git cat-file` to obtain object information. Whenever such a database is destroyed, the persistent commands will be shut down.

To use it, just instantiate your `Repo` instance as follows:
```python
import git
r = git.Repo(odbt=git.GitCmdObjectDB)
r.commit().message
# now lsof shows some pack files
del r
# now they are gone
```

It's a bit sad that there seems to be no way to trigger this kind of resource release in the pure-python implementation, and considering that GitPython is in maintenance mode, I believe this is not going to improve.

Please let me know if the suggested workaround did indeed work for you.
Thanks

Convolution using FFT is not necessarily faster than ordinary convolution. It depends on the relative sizes of the kernels. Both inputs must be large for FFT to be faster. If one input is ~ 1x1 compared to the other, than ordinary convolution has a time complexity of ~O(n), whereas FFTs time complexity is O(n log n). For optimal speed both methods should be considered.

ZYX/PRC notation is also unfortunate from a geometric standpoint, especially pertaining to n-dimensional quasipolar coordinates ([see section 2.2 for definition](https://digitalscholarship.unlv.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=3126&context=thesesdissertations)).

In this definition, `x1`, `x2`, and `x3` pertain to the `y`, `x`, and `z` dimensions, respectively. While alphabetically out of order, this makes sense if we look at how matrices are typically represented in terms of the xy-coordinate system: `row` corresponds to `y` and `column` corresponds to `x`. That is, a matrix `M` has a value `v = M[y, x]` at cartesian coordinates `(x, y)`. In other words, `y` is the first dimension, and `x` is the second dimension in matrices.

By extension, `x_n`corresponds to the `n`th dimension, making `z` the third dimension. It logically follows that in an xyz-coordinate system, a matrix `M` has a value `v = M[y, x, z]` at cartesian coordinates `(x, y, z)` and that additional entries should follow the form `Nr, Nc, [..., Np]`.

Placing planes before rows and columns disrupts this correspondence between dimensions ordering and their quasipolar coordinate  index. Besides it just generally being confusing and different from what most users are used to.

As far as I see you are mixing two concepts. You got the token which is designed to support REST authentication but then you probably use just normal django views and templates.

If you want to be sure that token is used properly then:

* get the token via login endpoint
* set the http [auth header](http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/authentication/#tokenauthentication) to each next following request (let's say ajax request)
* ensure that you set django rest framework authentication backend to [TokenAuthentication class](http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/authentication/#tokenauthentication)
* ensure that your django view inherits from [APIView class](http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/views/#class-based-views) from django rest framerwork.

This is a really good tutorial for building Python with Visual Studio:
http://p-nand-q.com/python/building-python-27-with-vs2010.html
And you need to do this because otherwise jnius throws an exception indicating that it can't find the python debug files.

DOWNLOAD - 
   Source files for the Python distribution you want to use. You are going to have to build the debug files - instructions to come. At the end you will still install the msi from Python directly, but you'll replace the files in the install directory with those you have built (See the tutorial above). Below you'll find some things that are not manifestly obvious from the tutorial.

INSTALL - 
   Visual Studio 2008 or higher
   MS Visual C++ 2010 Redistributable Package (x86)
              (www.microsoft.com/en-us/download/details.aspx?id=5555)
   Java JDK (I installed java 7.75) 
   GnuWin32 Make (http://gnuwin32.sourceforge.net/packages/make.htm)
   Python* (2.7.9 or higher comes with PIP installed)
              pip install cython==0.19.2
   *Wait to install python until the very end, after you have finished building the debug files from the tutorial above !!!

ADD ENVIRONMENT VARIABLES- 
- PYTHON_HOME (python install directory, C:\Python<ver.>)
- PYTHON_SCRIPTS (%PYTHON_HOME%\Scripts)
- PYTHONPATH (%PYTHON_HOME%; %PYTHON_HOME%\DLLs; %PYTHON_HOME%\Lib;   %PYTHON_HOME%\Tools; %PYTHON_SCRIPTS%;)
- JDK_HOME
- JRE_HOME
- JAVA_HOME (=JDK_HOME but make both)
- GNU_HOME (GnuWin32 install dir)

ADD TO path - 
   %PYTHON_HOME%; %PYTHON_SCRIPTS%; %JAVA_HOME%; %JAVA_HOME%\bin; %JAVA_HOME%\jre\bin\server; %GNU_HOME%\bin
++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++

I am seeing this still, and it's a bit confusing, but here is what is happening. We have this code in Sentry:

https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/1a0d697dd11459bfa8ce933b3b53264098dad60d/raven/utils/json.py#L46-L51

The logic here seems to be: Try to serialize everything to JSON, and if that fails for any reason, try it again, but this time use a special codec that ignores any errors. (Question: Why use a catch-all here, rather than just catching encoding-related exceptions?).

The problem, as stated above, is that on Python 3, the `encoding` argument is no longer supported. So if for any reason this fallback-branch triggers, we get an exception like this:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/raven/handlers/logbook.py", line 49, in emit
    return self._emit(record)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/raven/handlers/logbook.py", line 100, in _emit
    **handler_kwargs
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/raven/base.py", line 651, in capture
    self.send(**data)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/raven/base.py", line 746, in send
    message = self.encode(data)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/raven/base.py", line 788, in encode
    return zlib.compress(json.dumps(data).encode('utf8'))
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/raven/utils/json.py", line 63, in dumps
    return json.dumps(value, cls=BetterJSONEncoder, **kwargs)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/json/__init__.py", line 238, in dumps
    **kw).encode(obj)
TypeError: __init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'encoding'
```

So the task here would probably to a) find a way to support the fallback-branch on Python 3, or disable the fallback branch on Py3 so the exception is less confusing.

Regarding comments -- the easiest way is to leave them from the form at the bottom of the page in "Conversation" tab of the PR. Comments left as answers to in-line comments will eventually be collapsed out of view when the line they were assigned to changes.

Regarding my comment about `~Aggregator()`. I merely noticed that you declare it in the `aggregator.h` file, but then do not define in the `aggregator.cc` file. Normally this would compile but then fail at linking stage. However, your code doesn't fail at linking, which got me very surprised. I suspect the reason is that there is no place in the code where the destructor is invoked. Indeed, if you look at `pydatatable::aggregate()` function you'll see that you're creating Aggregate objects via `new Aggregate()`, but then never delete them. Thus, it's a resource leak.

As for `dt_in` and `dt_out` members of `Aggregate` -- there should be a clear understanding on who owns these pointers, i.e. who is required to free those resources once they are no longer needed. It looks like `Aggregate` is not owning them right now -- if that's intentional, then I suggest to leave a clarifying comment about the correct usage of the `Aggregate` class: in particular, what measures must the user take to ensure that the pointers do not become dangling.

There's a deeper problem with tower-cli options for surveys - there basically are none. After doing some testing of problems that @simfarm discovered, I realized that even if the job template sets `ask_variables_on_launch=true`, then it will show the existing extra_vars for that template. Survey variables don't get added to the list which is presented to the user. You have to know the survey variable names in advance (basically defeating the point of a survey). There is no way to view the survey questions in tower-cli, and including them would require additional navigation of the job template endpoints.

We could still use the existing structure of a click.edit() text input in a job launch. It would be straightforward to navigate the API endpoints go gather the survey questions and variable defaults. Then leave the questions as comments. I think users would still prefer an editable text area over a linear question/answer structure (although it would be good to get feedback on this). I would rank this as a top priority for a 2.4.0 release.

Et oui il faut en éliminer un (plutôt le premier) où faire le lien (inclure le premier dans le second mais on crée des variables qui ne servent un peu à rien).

Pour moi ce n'est pas acceptable. 

Une des spécificités d'openfisca est de gérer plusieurs entités pour faire des simulations de l'ensemble du système.

Je peux comprendre que des contributeurs qui ne s'intéressent qu'à des prestations sociales se concentrent sur le champ mais cela dégradera le logiciel. Ainsi, avec des données d'enquêtes nous utilisons l'entité ménage pour appariement afin de déterminer les loyers. 

Je pense que représenter fidèlement les ménages est important aussi pour les co-locations.

Par ailleurs France toute les stats publiques se font au niveau ménage, on ventile selon les loyers des ménages etc.

Enfin, la notion de famille est plus faible: chaque prestations à en fait un périmètre qui s'approche de notre famille mais qui est en fait différent (foyer social caf est différent du foyer social rsa). On gère cela comme on peut pour l'instant mais c'es un autre bonne raison pour ne pas faire de famille une unité trop centrale. La véritable avancée ce serait la génération des entités (famille ou foyer sociaux) adéquates pour les prestations sociales selon les conditions législatives.

Currently, when using Py-ART to plot radar data a single colormap (jet) and set of luminosity limits (-6 to 100) is used to generate the plot regardless of the field being visualized.  Specifying the cmap, vmin, and vmax arguments will override these defaults.  This is not an ideal situation.  The same colormap and luminosity limits should not be used for reflectivity data, Doppler velocities and other fields. 

We would like to change this behavior so that different colormaps and luminosity limits are provided for a number of known radar fields.  For example a "reflectivity" field will be plotted using the "pyart_NWSRef" colormap and limits of -30 to 75 where as a "velocity" field will use the "pyart_NWSVel" colormap with limits given by +/- the Nyquist velocity.  It will still be possible to override these defaults by specifying the cmap, vmin and vmax arguments and these defaults can be customized by [adjusting the Py-ART configuration file](http://arm-doe.github.io/pyart-docs-travis/user_reference/generated/pyart.load_config.html).

What we would like your help in is deciding on these default colormaps and luminosity limits for the various fields the Py-ART configuration file knows about.  Below is a table of proposed colormaps and limits, if you have an opinion on these please comment The [pyart_colormaps_and_limits repository](https://github.com/jjhelmus/pyart_colormaps_and_limits) has plots from a variety of radar for  many of these proposed colormaps and limits.

| reflectivity                          |   pyart_NWSRef            |   -30       |   75          |

HI @blakev,

I don't understand the need for this change. perhaps you could show an example test where the existing functionality makes things awkward and this feature would help?

Generally speaking, I think adding flow control (if/else/etc) statements to a test is a bad idea: It becomes harder to reason about what was tested by looking at the code. If you find yourself needing to do this, there's almost certainly a better way - perhaps by splitting the tests into two or more tests that test each path separately.

```python
        if self.implicit:

(1) With the current interface that only takes a row range, you don't know the row keys that still need to be read. You do know the last key that was read before an error (they come back in lexicographical order), which tells you which row range you need to send in the retried request. 

(2) I don't think re-implementing the mock server is Python is a great use of time. We can easily build linux, mac and windows binaries of the mock server and do something like pull one down from a public GCS bucket when the systests run depending on the current platform. I think the systests already install the bigtable emulator via gcloud which is along the same lines.

The shortcut here is to use the `BIGTABLE_EMULATOR_HOST` environment variable as described [here](https://cloud.google.com/bigtable/docs/emulator). The python library has support for it and *should* solve this problem for us. The mock retry server is essentially the emulator with different guts.

- [`quotaUser`](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/json_api/v1/parameters#quotaUser) (enforce per-user quotas from a server-side application even in cases when the user's IP address is unknown)
- [`userIp`](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/json_api/v1/parameters#userIp) (enforce per-user quotas when calling the API from a server-side application.)

Best place to start is probably inspecting the XML Word produces for cell shading. `opc-diag` is a handy tool for that sort of thing. There are a couple ways it might go, basically the simple way and the more powerful way, but we probably want to start with modeling the mechanism Word uses by default. That way we establish both the read and write capability at the same time.

Once that's established I can guide better. Offhand though I expect python-pptx will be a better model. The architecture is virtually identical and the two file formats share a lot of the same XML structure for things like this.

You might want to have a look here at the AutoShape.fill property:
https://github.com/scanny/python-pptx/blob/master/pptx/shapes/autoshape.py#L304

which constructs a FillFormat object:
https://github.com/scanny/python-pptx/blob/master/pptx/dml/fill.py#L20

which via an intermediary or two ends up in the oxml layer here to manipulate the XML:
https://github.com/scanny/python-pptx/blob/master/pptx/oxml/shapes/shared.py#L265

This general method is the "more complex but more powerful" method, so we'll need to see what Word does and then have a think on what would be the best initial approach.

If you can isolate an XML specimen or two that shows a shaded cell (with minimum context) and paste it into a reply post that will get us started.

You can refer to the ffmpeg documentation for setting the output encoding. But note that the point of ffmpeg is to compress the frames (spatially and temporally), and the interpolation is likely part of the decoding step by the video viewer. If you want less lossy video, try e.g. GIF or SWF. Or TIFF/NPZ/BSDF if you have no need to view the video as a video (i.e. only storage of the frames).

I'd recommend applying vmin and vmax yourself using some math on the numpy arrays that represent the frames.

No, I wasn't going to do that. It would be great if you started that, just as long as you don't touch the doc files, so we don't have too many conflicts and I will completely stay out of the source files then.

One thing though, about Napoleon, that we need to coordinate is about documentation of class instantiation parameters. If you use auto-module without extra options, the documentation will not show up any special methods (including __init__). The Napoleon style guide allows either of documenting the instantiation args in the __init__ docstring or in the class docstring. But if they are in the __init__ then we should use the special-members options to automodule. This may not be an issue, as we also implement other special methods, that we may want to document. But the thing about where to keep instantiation arguments is more of an opinion/taste kind of thing. Initially, I though is was very ugly to keep that documentation in the class docstring, but the more I though about (these args being not only the args for __init__ but also for __new__) the more it started to make sense. Also of less importance, is that Sphinx already shows a call signature on the class name, which may means that it would make more sense to put them there. What is your take on this?

The issue with this is that there _is_ a subtle distinction between `requirements.txt` and `setup.py` (or at least in how I have interpreted their use), specifically that `requirements.txt` should be for development and `setup.py` for use as a library. The other thing enforcing this separation is that you can't have dependencies pinned to git commits in setup.py (it breaks the install), but this way around, we can't temporarily pin libraries to git commits because they are installed from `setup.py`.
[This probably describes it better.](https://packaging.python.org/discussions/install-requires-vs-requirements/#install-requires-vs-requirements-files)

I somewhat disagree about having shared endpoints being a separate CRUD API being a pain for clients. I think we should either completely unify them, or completely split them off.
The current half-combined state is, I think, the worst of all possible worlds for clients. They need to cope with shared endpoints being endpoints in their model, but then remember to use a different creation resource.
I think this is much more of a pain than an entirely separate API -- although I agree that there is a good model in which they are part of the same set of resources.

My own inclination is to have a single set of REST resources for all endpoint types, but omit many, many of the fields that we're returning for endpoints for which they don't apply.
For a shared endpoint or a GCP endpoint, the signal-to-noise ratio is so poor that I'm tempted to make wrapper classes that extract only the desirable fields in order to clarify what is important.

I think we need to consider a complete revision of endpoint documents, restructured to fit the growing complexity here. Shared Endpoints could fit into that model very well.

For example, this would be nice to see:
```yaml
# base endpoint document
{
  "DATA_TYPE": "endpoint",
  "endpoint_type": ...,
  "endpoint_id": "...",
  "display_name": "...",
  ...
}
# shared endpoint
{
  "DATA_TYPE": "endpoint",
  "endpoint_type": "share",
  "endpoint_id": "...",
  "display_name": "...",
  "host_endpoint_id": "...",
  "host_path": "...",
  ...
}
# gcp endpoint
{
  "DATA_TYPE": "endpoint",
  "endpoint_type": "globus_connect_personal",
  "globus_connect_setup_key": "...",
  ...
}
```

Right now, I have `gcp_connected` fields on my shared endpoints. To me, as a consumer of the API, that's not just inconvenient -- it's wrong. It states that "this is a meaningful field for all endpoints", when it clearly isn't.

I think we should either consider a special field like `endpoint_type`, or a generalized version of that, like
```yaml
{
  "DATA_TYPE": "xyz",
  "SUBTYPE": "yyz",
  ...
}
```

The notion of `endpoint_type="share"` is, I think, consistent with the model of Shares being first class entities, with a dual notion that "All Shares are valid Endpoints".

@bd4 I think we've left behind the CLI-specific discussion a bit. Would you like to continue this elsewhere as a discussion of the Transfer API?

I think we settled pretty quickly on the idea of `globus transfer share (create|show|delete|...)` as our way of interacting with shared endpoints.

A few questions remain that I think need resolution.

- Should the CLI expose the fact that you're working with an endpoint definition explicitly? This will, of course, be clear to anyone who looks at the API responses. It's a question of using `--share-id` vs. `--endpoint-id` to display a shared endpoint.
- When shared endpoint commands are used on non-shared endpoints, should the CLI throw an error, or just warn?
- When normal endpoint commands are used on shared endpoints, should the CLI throw an error, wran, or do nothing special?

Importantly, how does this impact the existing ACL commands like `acl list`?
If we start using `--share-id`, should `globus transfer acl list` take a `--share-id` or an `--endpoint-id`?
It's important to know that Shares are a type of Endpoint, and that `--share-id`s are valid `--endpoint-id`s, and although that intuitively argues for using `--endpoint-id` everywhere, I think it's very confusing to read `globus transfer share show --endpoint-id`.

I would add hooks to pass in the correct path type requested, i.e. cygwin, msys2, or unix.

The os.path module can return a absolute normalized path, from the there I find some regular expressions are considerably less ass pain than any other option. You are being helped greatly by the drive naming rules on windows. 

```python
import os
import re
from enum import Enum

class PathType(Enum):
    CYGWIN = 0,
    MSYS2  = 1

class PathConversion:
    drive_regex = re.compile('(?P<drive_letter>[A-Z]):\\\\(?P<path>.*)')
    msys2_drive = '/{drive_letter}/{path}'
    cygwin_drive = '/cygdrive/{drive_letter}/{path}'
    @classmethod
    def normpath(self,x,pt=PathType.MSYS2):
        m = self.drive_regex.match(os.path.abspath(x))
        if m:
            dr_letter = m.group('drive_letter')
            path = m.group('path').replace('\\','/')
            if pt==PathType.MSYS2:
                path = self.msys2_drive.format(drive_letter=dr_letter.upper(), path=path)
            else:
                path = self.cygwin_drive.format(drive_letter=dr_letter.lower(),path=path)

            return path

if __name__ == "__main__":
    x = 'C:/home/workspace_cdt'
    pt = PathType.CYGWIN

    p = PathConversion.normpath(x,pt)

    print(p)
```

You can do

    conda config --set max_shlvl 1

The default value in conda 4.4 is `2` for `max_shlvl` because it emulates what had effectively been the behavior in conda 4.3 and below, where the base environment is put on `PATH` by manually setting e.g. `export PATH="/opt/conda/bin:$PATH"` rather than `source /opt/conda/bin/activate root`.  After that, `source activate` would add one additional environment on top of the base environment, but not more.  That behavior was preserved in conda 4.4.

The changes look good.

The tests run by travis work with previously recorded http interactions, which are saved in the tests/recordings subfolder.

When adding a new test, you'll need to run the test once in recording mode.  This will create its recording file (one per test).  You commit that file to the repository.

By default, tests are run in playback mode.  To change this, create a file named `testsettings_local.json` in the tests folder with the following content:
```
{
    "mode" : "Record"
}
```

Valid values for `mode` are: `RunLiveNoRecord`, `Playback`, `Record`.  If you ever need to overwrite an existing recording, you'll need to delete the recorded file first.

The bad news is that I think you can only record when using requests with token authentication, which means setting up Azure Active Directory, which can be tricky.  There are some resources online for that though.

In your `legacy_mgmt_settings_real.py`:

1. Set `CONNECTION_TYPE = CONN_TYPE_REQUESTS_TOKEN`.
2. Implement `get_token()`.

You can use ADAL for this, there are a few helper functions that call into ADAL in azure-common/testutils/common_auth.py, such as get_token_from_username_password or get_token_from_client_credentials.

There's also an example here on how to get the token for username/password without ADAL: https://azure-sdk-for-python.readthedocs.org/en/latest/resourcemanagementauthentication.html

Hi @GMLudo,

Unfortunately, retrieve the TTL as a related metadata of a DNS query can end up being a mess, the default implementation of `getaddrinfo` relays on the C implementation that implements for POSIX systems the most common RFC, including as a response a limited structure [1]. I would say that using `aiodns` and avoiding the naive `gethostbyname` implementation and using the raw access to the `cares` via the `query` function it should be possible.

IMHO the current commit avoids all of this complexity just adding a new user parameter, with that the user has the chance to avoid having wrong entries in his DNS cache and also taking advantage of the cache yet. In fact, I would prefer to put some value such as 60 seconds per default ... but it could create too much controversial because of breaking change.

There is a discussion also open in the Tornado repo about that [2]

Yes, If I'm not wrong a `Session` caches the entries forever. Use the same session for a long time can end up having DNS entries that are not valid, perhaps if the services are behind an AWS ELB [3] 

[1] http://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man3/getaddrinfo.3.html
[2] https://github.com/tornadoweb/tornado/pull/626
[3] https://aws.amazon.com/articles/1636185810492479

## Should we port container (and row and col) from bootstrap?
I think we should rather create blocks for each real use case:

- Even split (50% for each column).
- 60/40 split.
- 40/60 split.
- ... and so on

This has a couple of advantages:

- We have full control of mobile rendering per theme. A theme can override the default (what should be first on mobile).
- We limit how badly developers can fuck up (diverge from the intent of the designer).

I think we should have mixins for various layouts, and some specific classes that we can override in themes (and replace with another mixin or completely custom css that may handle mobile rendering differently).
Since this patch uses `arrow`, it is better to stay out of library. Please create an appropriate issue you're facing and then we can discuss on the solution.

@menudoproblema If you want to skip dates, you should make an `rruleset` (as I was showing above) with `rruleset.exdate` or `rruleset.exrule`. There's no need to modify `rrule` for that purpose.

Also, not sure what the unfathomable interactions are about dates and times.  If you don't specify anything to do with the time aspects, it will use whatever the time portion of `dtstart` is. For `exrule`s, the date will only be excluded from the `rruleset` if it's an exact match, so if you create an `exdate` of `datetime(2016, 12, 27, 17, 30)`,  it won't match a daily recurrence that occurs at 11:42.

If you don't care about the time aspects at all, then you may want to just create a helper function like this:

```python
from datetime import datetime, time
def to_midnight(dt):
    return datetime.combine(dt, time(0, 0))
```

Then you can either add that in as a wrapper in an `rruleset` subclass or just apply it to anything you're going to pass to `rdate` or `exdate`, and apply it to `dtstart` as well.

I know you like using methods where functions might otherwise work.  In most cases I cringe a little but recognize it doesn't really hurt anything.  This is a case where I'll ask you to consider using functions where possible.

There is eventually going to be a C/Cython version of this.  Because perf is critical here, I'm guessing we'll end up going full-on C.  Once there are two versions, we'll need to be super-careful to keep them in sync.  Two things we can do to keep them in sync are 1) fine-grained unit tests to ensure their behaviors are the same and 2) keep the code structure parallel in each version.  Using functions instead of methods makes this _much_ easier, especially in the raw-C case.

Why not take the private versions of these methods and turn them into functions?  The instance/class attributes that are accessed can be passed as args.

If you pin to `python-dateutil<5.0.0` on your app that supports both Python 2 and 3, your Python 2 users will get something from the 3.x line and your Python 3 users would get something from the 4.x line, assuming they use a `pip` that supports `Python-Requires`.

If you have an app or library that is Python 2-only, you'd pin to `python-dateutil<4.0.0`.

Here's what the method definitions might look like.  I'm probably missing details, as I haven't delved into all the combinations the api currently presents.

* `pre_load(self, data)` gets the raw data and returns data to be deserialized.  It only ever gets one item at a time, removing the need to know if many is True.  (Internally the schema calls it for each item if many is True.)
* `post_load(self, data)` gets the validated, deserialized data and returns the final data/object.  Like `pre_load`, it gets one item at a time.
* `pre_dump` and `post_dump` work the same way
* `validate(self, data, errors)` performs schema-level validation, after all fields are validated individually during (de)serialization.  It puts messages in the errors dict, and returns nothing.  Internally, the schema knows if there are errors now by checking if there are values in the error dict.  This also means that there's no separate "external" `validate` vs. `load` methods, just call `load` and check `errors is not None`.
* `error_handler` is removed.  Whatever it does can be done in `validate`.
* `validate_<field name>(self, value)` gets the value *after* it has been (de)serialized by the field, to perform extra validation.  This is a convenience *in addition to* specifying a list of validators on the field.

The behavior of `validate` and `validate_<field name>` is more in line with how Django and WTForms do things.

The logic for dump or load looks like this: `pre_` → `(de)serialize` → `validate` (schema level) → `post_` (only if no errors).  Within `(de)serialize`, the logic for each field is: `(de)serialize` → `validate_<field name>`.

This means there's no way to return data *and* errors at the same time.  Either the data was valid and errors is None, or the data was invalid and is None, and there are errors.  This avoids getting data in some indeterminate state where some of it is valid, some of it has a default/None applied because of errors, etc.

> Well, if validation can only ever return either an error or the valid data, then there's not a lot of point in returning both data and errors in any event.

Yes there is, you need to know *which* of the two were returned.  The other option you advocate is basically always setting `strict=True`.

> currently schema contexts are only applied for serialization, not for deserialization

That's the entire point of this bug report: the dump and load apis are not symmetrical.

First, while I don't want to break the API for fun, if it needs to change for the long term health of the library then we should change it. We've broken user code in every 0.x release and I don't intend to stop until we decide on a 1.0. So, don't feel too constrained by the past.

Next, when making the [new API docs](http://pvlib-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html), it felt pretty natural to break up the irradiance and pvsystem sections into subsections. That's probably a clue that the modules themselves should be chopped up too, though you could certainly take issue with some of the function classifications. I didn't make subsections for atmosphere or clear sky, and I don't think I'd support subpackages for them at this point either. I can imagine a solarposition subpackage that contains spa.py, a new ephemeris.py, and the remaining functionality in core.py. modelchain is long and complicated, but it does something complicated that I don't know how to do otherwise. Some of forecast might end up in iotools.

Concerning implementation, pandas and some other pydata packages use ``api.py`` modules instead of ``__init__.py`` modules to control their namespaces. I don't know the reasoning behind this other than the "``__init__.py`` should be empty" recommendation, but we should probably look into it.

Finally, we could wait and see if the subpackage approach for io tools actually leads to more people contributing more easily maintained code.

The BondOrientationalOrder class is indeed very elegant and it would make sense to start by doing something similar for bispectrum of hyperspherical harmonics on 3-sphere in R^4 (HSH). 

Unfortunately, I could not quite make sense of the expression for HSH [as products of Legendre functions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spherical_harmonics#Higher_dimensions).  The expression in R^n due to Atsushi specializes correctly for n=3 (spherical harmonics) but when I try it for n=4 (HSH) I get [this expression](https://github.com/hackingmaterials/matminer/files/2338352/atsushi_01.pdf). Here is the latex source:
$Y_{l_1l_2l_3}(\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3) = Y_{l_2}^{-l_1}(\theta_2, -\theta_1) \sin^{-\frac{1}{2}}(\theta_3) P_{l_3+\frac{1}{2}}^{-l_2-\frac{1}{2}}(\cos\theta_3)$
The problem is that the indices to the Legendre function are half integer, which does not make sense to me.  Maybe I am misunderstanding something in Atsushi's notation.  I looked in the very extensive handbook by Varshalovich, Moskalev, Khersonskii (VMK), but did not see any formula resembling Atsushi's.

The only practical way I know to compute a particular HSH is to generate the full set up to some order using the recursion method implemented in LAMMPS, based on formulae given in VMK Section 4.8.2

The initial concept that Ben and I came up with was precisely not having to define properties in terms of populations. So, there doesn't necessarily have to e on cellParam per population. The user can chose to make the same cellParam rule apply to several populations; or several cellParam rules to subsets of a population. Cells have a set of tags (popLabel, cellModel, cellType, x,y,z etc) and its up to the user to specify how to set properties based on these. I understand it s a bit loose, but its also much more flexible, which is what we were looking for. In most cases the user will decide to apply one cellParam rule per cellType or popLabel, but we don't want to necessarily enforce that. 

If the user adds two cellParam rules with the same conditions (eg. cellType and CellModel) they will both be applied in sequential order, overwriting the previous one where overlaps happen. Using exactly the same conditions doesnt' obviously make sense and netpyne could detect and issue an error/warning. However, it is conceivable that the user may want to apply a general rule to all cellType: 'IT', and then, using a second rule, modify a single specific parameter (eg. channel conductance) of all cells within cortical depth [200,400]. These 2 rules may end up applying to the same cell (cellType IT with y [200,400]), and thats the kind of flexibility we wanted to allow. 

Internal checks can be added to make sure that eg. all cells match at least one of the cellParam rules.

@omaciel I'm thinking of having a lower bar than that for getting commits merged, and increasing the scope of what a reviewer might do in their follow-up commit. I really do want to make sure that the requirements for having a pull request considered and eventually merged are clearly laid out and reasonably low, so as to avoid stepping back into our current situation of having frustratingly hard code reviews.

This can lead to a perverse incentive where a submitter meets only the minimum requirements for a pull request and puts a large burden on the pulp smash gatekeepers. Stating "you pull request is more likely to be reviewed merged in a prompt manner if ..." puts that incentive back in place. It gently encourages submitters to reach for the sky by making clear that reviewers have limited time and resources.

Thanks for raising this issue and working to address it.

As for handling multiple responses, you point out the right issues.  One nit is that the Thread spec does not specify how many responses a device must be able to support.  A max queue length of 1 is still a valid.  That said, OT could provide a compile-time configurable option the number of responses to support.  If you want to pursue this path, static allocation seems best.  In the future, we could better segment OT's "pre-commissioned" and "post-commissioned" memory and have them share the same memory region.

One other nit on the Thread spec is its use of the term "exact match" - Bloom filters are probabilistic data structures and do not provide "exact matches".

As I said, some SOCs do not provide pin observably.  

Your suggestion was the SPI-SLAVE holds MISO as an additional handshake. The problem occurs on SPI-MASTER side, example: PIN(X) is in mode GPIO, or PIN(X) is in mode (MISO).  For some SOCs when the pin is in a NON_GPIO mode reading the raw pin value (ie: as if it was a GPIO) is not possible due to the way the hardware was designed.

How then will the host know the slave is ready?

Yeah, I agree with you in the sense we should keep some sort of structure and not shove everything into `napalm`. So we should have things like:

```
napalm.base # for common things
napalm.eos # for eos related stuff
napalm.exceptions # for exceptions
...
```

`get_network_driver` and `SUPPORTED_DRIVERS` are probably the exception and can be just returned in the root namespace.

I am not so sure about `from napalm.base.utils import py23_compat`. I think that's an internal import, that's not part of the public API so we shouldn't have it in there. I think we should have in there only what we expect people to import use in their code.

I actually think we should extend this to `base`, `eos`, `ios`, etc... so we can clearly indicate what's part of the public API and what not. That way these two should be equivalent:

```
# neither of these two should let people use things we don't consider "public"
from napalm import eos  
import napalm.eos
```

So if people does:

```
from napalm.eos import something_not_public
```

it's up to them and completely at their own risk.
Also regarding the documentation it mentions `from napalm import get_network_driver` should that be supported or changed to `from napalm.base import get_network_driver`?

Don't do that: inheriting from most stl classes isn't a good idea (very few classes in the stl are polymorphic). Yes, I understand that, but I actually have spent some days already researching why long running requests from Chrome (didn't see it with `curl`) can cause multiple (± every 2min) requests to be "seen" by the REST server, and it has even caused multiple deposits and transfers to be performed.
- Proper solution: find out why that happen and fix it. That way, we can support arbitrarily long requests on the API. I've a bet it has something to do with keep-alive packets, 
- Workaround 1: Idempotent behavior on all state-changing endponts.
- Workaround 2: Timeout on client, to avoid triggering that bug.
Correct :)

Another thing to note is that if the timeout does occur and the transfer is considered failed then we still modify our `transferred_amount` in the channel.

Is this desired behaviour?

For example, if I try to send 1 token to Andre 3 times and he is offline or his uPnP is not working and is unreachable I will still have my balance set to `Deposit - 3` and not `Deposit - 1`. Once he fixes it and comes back online if I try to send him the transfer that was failing, so an 1 token transfer, and if he receives it he will receive `4` tokens and my balance will be `Deposit - 4`

In some scenarios this would be desirable behaviour, but would it always make sense? What if we have a direct channel with someone and we send him 1 token to buy a real life product and he says he did not receive it due to network errors? 

We can't retry. Retrying would mean that we would send him 2 times the amount.

So the question here is:

Should the transferred amount be modified once the direct transfer is sent (current behaviour) or only once the ACK is received?

The environment variables on the workers are inherited from the local scheduler or raylet process that creates them, which in turn is created by the `ray start` command (or the `ray.init()` command), so if you set the environment variable before calling `ray start` (on each machine), then it will work (or before starting the Python process if you are calling `ray.init()`.

Whenever a worker executes a task, we could in theory swap in the environment variables for that task's job and then swap them back out when the next task runs. However, a lot of these environment variables only matter at certain points in time (e.g., setting `MKL_NUM_THREADS` doesn't help after importing numpy I think, and the workers may have imported numpy long before the job is executed).

So for this proposal to make sense, we probably also need to not share workers between different jobs (which is probably a good idea anyway).

@istoica 
I think this API should only focus on generic status (in system level, e.g., job state, number of tasks/objects, etc). This is used for monitoring job's healthiness and managing jobs.

It will be much more complex to also include application-level status (e.g., training accuracy), because 1) as you mentioned, we'll need to provide an API to let users define their metrics; 2) users need to tell us how to aggregate and display the metrics.
For application-level metrics, it makes sense to let users collect and process the metrics by themselves.

> Maybe you can you advise here how co configure the GUI?

The suggested workaround doesn't work this way in the Twitch GUI, because it expects a single executable path which gets validated first (is-file and is-executable). You're seeing an ENOENT error (no entity / non-existing file), because it interprets the whole string as a single file. What you can do, is setting the player to "open" (the macOS specific GUI app launcher tool) and use the --player-args parameter, like this:

```bash
streamlink --player "open" --player-args " -W -n -a '/Applications/QuickTime Player.app/Contents/MacOS/QuickTime Player' --args '{filename}'" --player-passthrough hls URL QUALITY
```
Notice the space at the beginning of the `--player-args` parameter value or else you'll encounter the error described in #971. `-W` lets `open` wait for the spawned process to exit, which is a requirement here. `-n` lets it always launch a new player instance.

I'm not sure though if this will work, but that's the correct way of preparing the player launch parameters in Streamlink. Unfortunately, I can't test this myself, since I'm unable to watch videos in my macOS VMs because of lack of GPU acceleration.

This issue seems to pop up when pip is installing discord.py[voice]. (I ran into it trying to use python 3.7... which FYI doesn't work for other reasons, so I had to downgrade to 3.6)

Here is a workaround I found.

1. Manually download discord.py from 
https://files.pythonhosted.org/packages/97/3c/2a97b47fd8839f8863241857bbd6a3998d1de1662b788c8d9322e5a40901/discord.py-0.16.12.tar.gz
2. Edit this line in the Red-DiscordBot requirements.txt
`git+git://github.com/Rapptz/discord.py.git#egg=discord.py[voice]`
to point to the downloaded directory with [voice] at the end.  For example:
`C:\Users\you\Desktop\discord.py-0.16.12[voice]`
3. Edit this line in setup.py in the discord.py directory
```
extras_require = {
    'voice': ['PyNaCl==1.0.1'],
}
``` 
To
```
extras_require = {
    'voice': ['PyNaCl==1.2.1'],
}
```
4. Run start_launcher.bat again from your Red-DiscordBot directory

Some discussion of what the `always_abort` thing is trying to do here: https://gitter.im/python-trio/general?at=5b77863e802bc42c5f356669

Interesting realization: the fundamental problem is that when there are multiple ways that a task can wake back up, it can be difficult for one path to know how to clean up the state used by the other paths. In this case, `put_handle.put` can be woken by either `get_handle.get` or `put_handle.close`. Other examples where I've brushed against this are similar: `Condition.wait` / `ParkingLot.repark` involves a task that goes to sleep on one queue and then later gets moved to another, so it's challenging for its `abort_fn` to keep track of which queue it should be aborted from. #242 discusses the case where we sleep on an arbitrary collection of different events, and then of course we need something clever indeed to keep track of all the different wakeup paths.

I don't think `always_abort` is really working. In that discussion though I realized a much simpler hack that would work for both this case, and for `ParkingLot.repark`, is to add a bit of state attached to the task object that the sleeper and waker are free to use to pass data back and forth. Maybe `task.sleep_state`, which as far as the trio core is concerned is meaningless except that it gets reset to `None` every time a task is rescheduled. Of course this is also potentially an argument for a more structured solution like #242, but we don't need to solve all that right now...
